verses.text: Joshua sent out from Shittim two men to spy secretly: Go view the land, even Jericho. They came into a harlot's house named Rahab, and lodged there. Guidance: Two spies, not twelve — Joshua learns from Numbers 13. And the safe house is the most unsafe address in town: a harlot's. The first foothold in Canaan is a woman of the wrong tribe, the wrong trade, with the right faith. God's reconnaissance often runs through people no committee would have picked.

The king of Jericho heard: men of Israel are come to search the country. He sent to Rahab: Bring forth the men. She hid them on the roof under stalks of flax, and said: They went out about dark, the gate was about to shut, pursue quickly. The pursuers went toward Jordan, the gate was shut behind them. Guidance: Rahab's lie protects God's messengers. Scripture does not moralize about her deception — it commends her faith (Heb 11:31, James 2:25). She had a roof, flax to spare, and a quick tongue. She used what she had to side with the LORD.

Before they slept she came to the roof: I know the LORD hath given you the land. Your terror is fallen upon us, all the inhabitants faint because of you. We have heard how the LORD dried up the Red sea, what ye did unto Sihon and Og. Our hearts did melt: the LORD your God, he is God in heaven above, and in earth beneath. Guidance: A Canaanite harlot quotes Deuteronomy 4:39 nearly word for word. Forty years of news has soaked through walls. While Israel doubted at Kadesh, Jericho was already terrified. The mission of the spies is over before it begins — Rahab's confession is the report.

Swear by the LORD: as I have shewed you kindness, shew kindness unto my father's house. Give me a true token: ye will save alive my father, mother, brethren, sisters, all they have, and deliver our lives from death. The men answered: Our life for yours, if ye utter not this our business. When the LORD hath given us the land, we will deal kindly and truly with thee. Guidance: Rahab bargains not for herself but for her whole household. Faith always widens its arms. The spies swear by the LORD — covenant language, not transaction. Salvation crosses the wall in both directions: she saves them by night, they will save her by day.

She let them down by a cord through the window: her house was upon the wall, she dwelt upon the wall. Get you to the mountain, lest the pursuers meet you; hide three days, until pursuers be returned: afterward go your way. Guidance: Her window is escape route and gospel symbol. Her house is built into the very wall that is about to fall — and yet hers will stand. The same wall that condemns the city saves her family. She tells them where to hide because she knows her own town better than they do.

The men said: We will be blameless of thine oath. Bind this line of scarlet thread in the window. Bring thy father, mother, brethren, all thy father's household home unto thee. Whosoever goes out of the doors into the street, his blood upon his head; whosoever shall be with thee in the house, his blood on our head. She said, So be it. She bound the scarlet line in the window. Guidance: A scarlet thread in the window — a quiet echo of the lamb's blood on the doorpost. The pattern of salvation repeats: a sign, a house, a covering, a door not to leave. Stay inside, and you live. Rahab obeys at once: the thread is up before the army arrives.

They abode three days in the mountain until the pursuers returned: the pursuers found them not. The two men returned to Joshua son of Nun, told him all that befell them: Truly the LORD hath delivered into our hands all the land; for all the inhabitants do faint because of us. Guidance: Their report is the opposite of Numbers 13. Same language ('the LORD hath given'), opposite spirit. Forty years between the two reports — the difference is leaders who believed Caleb's view. Rahab's confession travels back across the Jordan as the army's marching orders.
